narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record and plans, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40—below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 27, which is notably lower than the average 36.70, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2nd and 3rd deciles, respectively, compared to averages around the 5th or 6th. It also factored in your intention to attend part-time, a choice made by only about 7% of students, unlike the vast majority who go full-time. Overall, the model estimated a 56% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, tipping the scales just enough to recommend rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your plan to study part-time, as the model sees this less common path as a stronger risk for bar success compared to full-time commitment. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA, which the model views as a moderate signal pulling toward potential struggles on the bar, given how it stacks up against stronger averages. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income provided the strongest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward passing by suggesting some supportive resources not always available to others. While your LSAT and early grade deciles played roles, these top three elements dominated the decision, with part-time status having the biggest sway toward rejection. This outcome reflects how your profile compares to typical admitted students, who often have higher test scores, GPAs, and full-time plans that align with proven bar-passing patterns. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid family support, but this is just one data-driven snapshot—not a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, exploring ways to strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses, or committing to full-time study if feasible, then reapply to schools with more flexible criteria. You might also seek bar prep programs tailored for part-time students to build a stronger case next time. With targeted improvements, your path to law school remains very much open.
